title: Observability Cardinality Explosion Detection
dataSources: Splunk Observability Cloud metric ingest with sourcetype=o11y:metric and related HTTP Event Collector routes; Prometheus /metrics text and remote-write forwards into index=prometheus; OpenTelemetry Collector internal metrics including otelcol_exporter_sent_metric_points, otelcol_processor_dropped_metric_points, and otelcol_processor_batch_send_size in index=otel_metrics; Grafana Cloud or Datadog cardinality reports mirrored into Splunk via scheduled exports or add-on pulls; Splunk index=_metrics for platform metric store series; Splunk ITSI metric usage and KPI maintenance logs when KPIs amplify cardinality; custom HEC payloads from cardinality estimator sidecars; index=splunk_o11y_audit for limit and planner messages when exported.
app: Splunk Observability Cloud Add-on (splunk_app_o11y_cloud), Splunk Add-on for OpenTelemetry, Splunk Add-on for Prometheus, Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit for forecast baselines, optional Splunk Edge Processor for cardinality reduction filters at ingest.
description: Detects observability cardinality explosion before it becomes an outage in the monitoring plane itself: high-cardinality dimensions such as request_id, user_id, session_id, raw URL paths, ephemeral pod identifiers in Kubernetes, A/B cohort tags, careless Prometheus relabel rules, or OpenTelemetry resource detectors that stamp node UIDs onto every pod series multiply unique label combinations. Each combination expands metric time series counts in Observability Cloud and other time-series backends, which can trigger ingest rejection, query planner timeouts, dashboard failures, and multi-million United States dollar spend spikes when commercial contracts bill on active series times retention. Unlike UC-13.5.16 SLO burn alerts, UC-13.5.12 detector health checks, UC-13.5.11 synthetic availability, UC-13.5.7 RED service-map rate-error-duration panels, UC-13.5.10 browser JavaScript error streams, or UC-13.5.9 Core Web Vitals user experience measurements—which all describe customer-visible outcomes—this control focuses on telemetry shape: per-metric estimated series counts, hour-over-window growth, policy comparisons from metrics_inventory.csv, and preventive processor or relabel actions that keep dashboards trustworthy and FinOps predictable.
value: Platform and FinOps leaders gain an early, evidence-backed signal when a team accidentally turns every checkout attempt or canary pod name into a new metric label, long before customer-facing SLO detectors move. Service owners receive concrete owner and team routing from metrics_inventory.csv instead of guessing who pays for a surprise bill. Reliability engineers avoid the vicious cycle where cardinality spikes force aggressive sampling that hides real incidents, because the search correlates OpenTelemetry processor drops, Prometheus federation proxies, Performance data model ingest volume, and optional Observability Cloud limit audit rows in one place. Legal and procurement reviewers keep a defensible narrative tying cardinality budgets to written policy_max_mts values rather than ad hoc finger pointing after quarter close.
grandmaExplanation: We watch whether your dashboards are remembering every tiny unique label like a receipt number on each measurement, because that habit makes charts crawl, bills balloon, and alarms cry wolf before anything your customers feel breaks. We help teams roll up the noise and keep the picture honest.
implementation: Land Observability Cloud, Prometheus, and OpenTelemetry internal metrics into Splunk metrics and event indexes; maintain metrics_inventory.csv with owners and policy_max_mts; deploy obs_uc_13_5_19_cardinality_explosion_sentinel hourly with earliest=-48h@h; page on CRITICAL and HIGH; pair Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit forecasts for baseline drift; optional Edge Processor drops for approved emergency label strips; archive weekly CSV and search exports to observability_evidence.
detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control jointly with observability platform engineers, FinOps partners, site reliability leaders, and service owners who sign cardinality budgets. Metric cardinality explosion is an anti-pattern where high-cardinality dimensions such as request_id, user_id, session_id, raw HTTP path segments, transient Kubernetes pod UIDs, A/B cohort labels, untrimmed Prometheus relabel rules, or OpenTelemetry resource detectors that emit node-level identifiers per pod leak into metric labels. Each new unique combination becomes another active series in your time-series database, which Observability Cloud represents as metric time series growth. The blast radius includes ingest rejection when org limits trip, query timeouts when scanners touch billions of series, dashboard failures when chart queries exceed planner budgets, and multi-million United States dollar cost explosions when commercial pricing multiplies series counts by retention windows. This use case is deliberately not UC-13.5.16 multi-window multi-burn-rate SLO alerting focused on customer-facing availability and latency outcomes, not UC-13.5.12 detector health narratives, not UC-13.5.11 synthetic probe availability, not UC-13.5.7 RED service-map fusion, not UC-13.5.10 real-user monitoring JavaScript errors, and not UC-13.5.9 Core Web Vitals. Those siblings track outcome signals about user experience and service health. UC-13.5.19 tracks the shape of the metric stream itself: estimated unique series counts per metric name, growth rates of those counts, top offending dimensions inferred from inventory policy, and rule-based prevention paths across collectors and relabel layers.

Begin with a cardinality lifecycle map. Instrumentation libraries emit raw measurements. OpenTelemetry Collector processors such as metricstransform, filter, cumulativetodelta, and batch reshape or drop labels before export. Prometheus relabel_configs and metric_relabel_rules on scrape or remote-write paths can strip or hash labels. Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector distributions ship cardinality guidance and processors aligned to vendor documentation. FedRamp and regulated tenants should pair technical controls with FinOps attestations because cardinality is both a reliability and a spend control. Maintain metrics_inventory.csv with columns metric_name, owner, team, allowed_dimensions, policy_max_mts, protected, and emergency_drop so searches join governance metadata without hard-coding team names in SPL. Tag ITSI entities when metric ownership ties to business services in your configuration management database. Size the Splunk _metrics index for projected dimensional cardinality and hot retention; undersized volumes force premature freezing or bucket churn that masquerades as application spikes. Train a Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit forecast helper model on approved low-cardinality baselines for a handful of platform metrics so you can compare naive growth to forecast bands during reviews. Publish an allow-list of dimensions that may legally appear on specific metrics, for example service, environment, region, and workload_class, and treat anything else as suspect until reviewed. Treat policy_max_mts as a service-level objective on cardinality itself, not as a soft suggestion.

RBAC and safety: readers of _metrics and prometheus indexes need metric read roles without edit indexes. Writers who mutate metrics_inventory.csv should use change tickets. Edge Processor or Collector operators who deploy automatic blocklists need separate approval workflows so emergency mitigation does not silently hide production signals.

Risk briefing: cardinality investigations are politically sensitive because they often reveal accidental per-user instrumentation in paths that product teams believed were anonymous aggregates. Expect pushback until you show dollars and query latency tied to concrete label keys.

Differentiation recap: per-metric estimated series counts, growth, inventory policy join, multisource fusion across Splunk metrics indexes, OpenTelemetry internal telemetry, Prometheus federation exports, Performance data model volume context, and optional Observability Cloud audit narratives, not SLO burn math, not detector heartbeats alone, not RUM vitals, not synthetic pass-fail rates.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Lane A lands Splunk metric points in index=_metrics with dimensional fields suitable for mstats. Preserve host, splunk_server, or instance style keys as stable stand-ins for the composite uniqueness dimension _dim that the search later deduplicates with dc. Why mstats instead of stats on raw events? Metric indexes store pre-aggregated measure columns and dimension maps; scanning raw events for the same cardinality math burns indexers and often truncates on busy clusters. mstats honors span and earliest windows against metric metadata without replaying full JSON payloads when configured correctly.

Lane B lands OpenTelemetry Collector internal metrics such as otelcol_exporter_sent_metric_points, otelcol_processor_dropped_metric_points, and otelcol_processor_batch_send_size plus broader otelcol_processor_* families into index=otel_metrics or equivalent. These series act as early warning when processors drop points or batch sizes explode during cardinality storms.

Lane C lands Prometheus federation or remote-write exports in index=prometheus with sourcetype patterns prometheus:metrics or prometheus:*. The search approximates cardinality pressure with dc(pod) per metric_name and hour as a pragmatic proxy when native cardinality APIs are not yet forwarded into Splunk. Replace pod with your highest-cardinality label only after reviewing federation overlap rules.

Lane D queries the Splunk Common Information Model Performance data model with tstats count grouped hourly to correlate general ingestion volume trends with metric storms. This arm is not a direct series counter; it contextualizes whether wider platform ingest is climbing in parallel with suspected cardinality leaks.

Lane E ingests Observability Cloud or hybrid access audit narratives into index=splunk_o11y_audit when your team exports limit messages mentioning metric time series caps, ingest gates, or planner refusals. Treat this arm as optional; keep it disabled if the index does not exist to avoid empty-arm confusion, or route those messages to a general audit index and adjust the search block accordingly.

HTTP Event Collector tokens that receive custom cardinality estimator jobs from sidecar tools should include tenant and realm fields to align with coalesce ladders on metric_name and tenant. Document Prometheus high availability pairs so federation jobs do not double count the same scrape targets into Splunk without dedupe hints.

props.conf and transforms.conf should normalize metric_name casing before CSV joins. metrics_inventory.csv must use lowercase metric_name keys to match the eval lower(trim()) ladder in the join subsearch.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as obs_uc_13_5_19_cardinality_explosion_sentinel with an hourly schedule in production analytics windows, dispatch earliest=-48h@h, dispatch latest=now, and alert when severity is CRITICAL or HIGH after verifying lookup coverage. Throttle duplicate metric_name and tenant pairs for forty-five minutes unless severity escalates. Include recommended_action, owner, and team columns in pager descriptions so incident commanders route to metric owners without ad hoc spreadsheet hunts.

Pipeline narrative: multisearch fans five arms for _metrics mstats uniqueness, OpenTelemetry internal telemetry sums, Prometheus federation distinct pod counts, Performance data model hourly counts, and Observability audit limit messages. coalesce unifies metric_name and tenant naming drift across arms. streamstats with a twenty-four hour time window estimates growth_rate_pct_h from min and max mts_count observed inside the sliding window per metric_name, tenant, and lane. eventstats derives org_p95_growth_rate and org_p50_growth_rate so a single noisy microservice does not drown contextual percentile baselines. join attaches metrics_inventory.csv for policy_max_mts, allowed_dimensions, protected, and emergency_drop governance. case maps CRITICAL when policy is exceeded by fifty percent and growth_rate_pct_h is at least ten, HIGH when above policy, MEDIUM at eighty percent utilization, INFO when emergency_drop allowlist flags the row, and LOW for protected or informational posture.

Fenced SPL must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-13.5.19 Cardinality explosion sentinel: _metrics series shape, OTel internal, Prometheus federation, Performance tstats, o11y audit. Tunables: earliest=-48h@h policy_default_mts=500000 growth_crit_pct_per_h=10")`
| multisearch
    [ | mstats latest(_value) AS mv WHERE index=_metrics earliest=-48h@h latest=now span=1h BY metric_name host
      | eval tenant=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(sf_organizationId,deployment_environment,tenant,"corp"))))
      | eval metric_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(metric_name,"unnamed"))))
      | eval _dim=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(host,splunk_server,instance,"nodim"))))
      | stats dc(_dim) AS mts_count BY _time metric_name tenant
      | eval lane="mstats_metrics_index" ]
    [ search index=otel_metrics earliest=-48h@h latest=now
        (metric_name="otelcol_exporter_sent_metric_points" OR metric_name="otelcol_processor_dropped_metric_points" OR metric_name="otelcol_processor_batch_send_size" OR LIKE(metric_name,"otelcol_processor_%"))
      | eval tenant=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(sf_orgId,org_id,tenant_id,"otel"))))
      | eval metric_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(metric_name,metricName,"otel_internal"))))
      | bin _time span=1h
      | stats sum(_value) AS lane_sum BY _time metric_name tenant
      | eval mts_count=round(lane_sum,4)
      | eval lane="otel_internal_metrics" ]
    [ search index=prometheus earliest=-48h@h latest=now (sourcetype=prometheus* OR sourcetype="prometheus:metrics")
      | eval metric_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(__name__,metric_name,"prom_metric"))))
      | eval tenant=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(cluster,k8s_cluster_name,prometheus_cluster,"prom"))))
      | bin _time span=1h
      | stats dc(pod) AS mts_count BY _time metric_name tenant
      | eval lane="prometheus_federation_cardinality" ]
    [ | tstats count FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-48h@h latest=now BY _time span=1h
      | eval metric_name="cim_performance_ingest_volume"
      | eval tenant="perf_datamodel"
      | eval mts_count=count
      | eval lane="tstats_performance_volume" ]
    [ search index=splunk_o11y_audit earliest=-48h@h latest=now ("MTS" OR "metric time series" OR "mts_limit" OR "ingest limit")
      | eval metric_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(o11y_metric,metric,signature,"o11y_limit_event"))))
      | eval tenant=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(org_id,realm,tenant,"o11y"))))
      | bin _time span=1h
      | stats count AS mts_count BY _time metric_name tenant
      | eval lane="o11y_access_audit" ]
| eval metric_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(metric_name,"unnamed_metric"))))
| eval tenant=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(tenant,"unknown_tenant"))))
| sort 0 metric_name tenant lane + _time
| streamstats time_window=24h global=f min(mts_count) AS mts_open max(mts_count) AS mts_shut BY metric_name tenant lane
| eval growth_rate_pct_h=if(mts_open>0, round(100*(mts_shut-mts_open)/mts_open,4), null())
| eventstats perc95(growth_rate_pct_h) AS org_p95_growth_rate perc50(growth_rate_pct_h) AS org_p50_growth_rate BY _time
| join type=left max=0 metric_name
    [| inputlookup metrics_inventory.csv
     | eval metric_name=lower(trim(toString(metric_name)))
     | fields metric_name owner team allowed_dimensions policy_max_mts protected emergency_drop ]
| eval policy_max_mts=tonumber(coalesce(policy_max_mts,500000))
| eval util_pct=round(100*mts_count/nullif(policy_max_mts,0),2)
| eval exceed_ratio=round(mts_count/nullif(policy_max_mts,0),4)
| eval severity=case(
    match(lower(toString(emergency_drop)),"yes|true|1|allowlist|allow"), "INFO",
    mts_count>(policy_max_mts*1.5) AND coalesce(growth_rate_pct_h,0)>=10, "CRITICAL",
    mts_count>policy_max_mts, "HIGH",
    util_pct>=80, "MEDIUM",
    match(lower(toString(protected)),"yes|true|1"), "LOW",
    true(), "LOW")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity=="CRITICAL", "page_finops_and_metric_owner_drop_labels_or_hash_dimensions_update_relabel_otel_processors",
    severity=="HIGH", "open_sev1_cardinality_triage_review_top_labels_and_prom_relabel",
    severity=="MEDIUM", "schedule_owner_workshop_compare_allowed_dimensions_to_live_labels",
    severity=="INFO", "document_emergency_drop_allowlist_no_page",
    true(), "monitor_inventory_gap_or_low_signal")
| table _time lane metric_name tenant mts_count growth_rate_pct_h org_p95_growth_rate org_p50_growth_rate util_pct exceed_ratio policy_max_mts severity owner team recommended_action allowed_dimensions
```

savedsearches.conf sketch:

```ini
[obs_uc_13_5_19_cardinality_explosion_alert]
cron_schedule = 0 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -48h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
enableSched = 1
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
search = | savedsearch obs_uc_13_5_19_cardinality_explosion_sentinel | where severity IN ("CRITICAL","HIGH")
```

Performance guidance: if Job Inspector shows multisearch queue time climbing, materialize hourly rollups of mts_count by metric_name and tenant into a summary index and widen operational alerts to read summaries while retaining this search for investigations.

Step 4 — Validate

Synthetic positive test: in a non-production tenant, deliberately add a request_id label to a test counter emitted through a lab OpenTelemetry pipeline, confirm the _metrics or prometheus arm shows a sharp mts_count climb within two collection intervals, and confirm obs_uc_13_5_19_cardinality_explosion_sentinel surfaces HIGH or CRITICAL when policy_max_mts in metrics_inventory.csv is set to a deliberately low laboratory ceiling. Remove the label leak via an OpenTelemetry filter processor or a Splunk Edge Processor drop rule, redeploy, and confirm severities return to LOW or informational within one hour.

Ground truth cross-check: compare Splunk mstats dc(_dim) outputs to vendor-native cardinality explorers in Observability Cloud, Grafana Cloud cardinality tools, or Datadog custom metric monitors for the same interval. Expect proportional—not identical—counts because Splunk may bucket by hour and vendors may snapshot at different times.

Lookup validation: run inputlookup metrics_inventory.csv alone and confirm every tier-one metric appears with realistic policy_max_mts values, non-zero for production services. Zero policy accidents are covered in troubleshooting.

Negative test: after cleanup, confirm the alert wrapper returns zero rows when filtering to CRITICAL and HIGH only on a steady cluster without intentional leaks.

Runbook linkage: tie pages to FinOps daily cardinality briefs, weekly owner reviews, ITSI Glass Table tiles that show cardinality posture beside RED metrics, and Splunk Edge Processor promotion pipelines for blocklists.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case A — mstats appears off acceleration or returns null: verify metrics index acceleration, confirm role inherits metrics_read, and reconcile index=_metrics name spelling versus metrics indexes on Cloud.

Case B — _metrics index sized too small: expand storage policy, reduce hot retention only after legal review, or route heavy dimensions to cold before muting alerts.

Case C — OpenTelemetry Collector not exporting internal metrics: enable telemetry pipelines in collector config, scrape the prometheus exporter on collectors, and confirm index=otel_metrics receives otelcol_processor lines.

Case D — Prometheus federation double counting between high availability pairs: dedupe by replica label, collapse federation jobs, or split HA scrape targets per written runbook.

Case E — exemplars or histogram buckets contaminating histogram cardinality: isolate exemplar labels, verify histogram metric names follow native conventions, and strip exemplar trace ids at collector when policy forbids them.

Case F — Machine Learning Toolkit forecast model stale: retrain baselines after known seasonality changes, pin training windows, and document model version in metrics_inventory notes column if added.

Case G — Owner lookup CSV missing rows: default severities should still fire but recommended_action should route to platform on-call until owners backfill CSV.

Case H — Splunk Edge Processor blocklist conflicts with allow-list dimensions: reconcile metrics_inventory allowed_dimensions with processor rules, enforce single source of truth, and test in shadow mode first.

Case I — policy_max_mts accidentally zero: guard eval uses coalesce to numeric default; fix CSV and replay search.

Case J — Kubernetes host.id resource attribute toggles across collector upgrades: expect short-lived duplicate series; smooth with rolling weekly baselines and annotate upgrades.

Case K — Prometheus relabel_configs regex unanchored: tighten patterns with anchors, add tests in CI for relabel snippets, and backport fixes before paging application teams.

Closing checklist: five em-dash step headers are present; Step 3 fenced SPL matches the spl field exactly; multisearch lists five arms; coalesce, streamstats, eventstats, case, join, inputlookup, mstats, and tstats appear; final table lists fifteen operational columns including growth and governance fields.


Appendix note for reviewers: cardinality governance is iterative; revisit allowed_dimensions after each major product launch, capture before-and-after screenshots in Observability Cloud or Grafana cardinality views, and archive weekly CSV snapshots of metrics_inventory.csv to a restricted evidence index for audit trails.

Appendix note for reviewers: cardinality governance is iterative; revisit allowed_dimensions after each major product launch, capture before-and-after screenshots in Observability Cloud or Grafana cardinality views, and archive weekly CSV snapshots of metrics_inventory.csv to a restricted evidence index for audit trails.

| sort 0 metric_name tenant lane + _time

| eval growth_rate_pct_h=if(mts_open>0, round(100*(mts_shut-mts_open)/mts_open,4), null())
| eventstats perc95(growth_rate_pct_h) AS org_p95_growth_rate perc50(growth_rate_pct_h) AS org_p50_growth_rate BY _time

cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=true count FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-48h@h latest=now BY Performance.process span=1h
| eval process_name=Performance.process
| stats sum(count) AS events BY process_name
| sort -events
| head 50
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Splunk CIM Performance summaries for hosts and processes feeding hourly tstats volume context beside metric cardinality arms; retain at least thirty days for quarter-over-quarter reviews.
visualization: Severity-colored table by metric_name and tenant with sparklines for mts_count, single-value tiles for org_p95_growth_rate, histogram of util_pct, and drilldowns to mstats, prometheus, otel_metrics, and audit lanes.

references.title: Splunk Observability Cloud — Control metric cardinality
Prometheus documentation — Instrumentation: do not overuse labels
Grafana Mimir — Planning capacity and cardinality
Datadog — Custom metrics without limits best practices
OpenTelemetry Collector Contrib — Metrics transform processor
Splunk Docs — Machine Learning Toolkit: Smart forecasting

knownFalsePositives: Legitimate cardinality growth appears during rolling deploys when new pod template hashes and canary names multiply series for an hour; require correlation with change tickets before paging owners. Seasonal retail bursts sometimes leak pseudo user identifiers into cohort labels during Black Friday experiments; finance-approved cohort keys may still be high-cardinality and should be whitelisted via emergency_drop only after legal review. Retroactive resampling or backfill pipelines can re-fan-out historical series and look like a live explosion until you segment _time. OpenTelemetry resource detector changes that add host.id then later remove it create short-lived duplicate identities; treat as migration noise bounded to upgrade windows. Prometheus federation from paired high-availability scrapers can double-count identical targets if dedupe labels differ; compare replica labels before blaming applications. Scheduled load tests that stamp synthetic user_id labels are expected; annotate test windows in metrics_inventory or suppress via protected flags. Histogram exemplars that intentionally attach trace identifiers are cardinal by design when misconfigured; distinguish exemplar policies from counter labels. ITSI KPI recalculations can temporarily inflate derived metric fan-out; cross-check ITSI maintenance modes. Edge Processor shadow rules that duplicate events during canary cutovers may inflate counts; validate processor metrics. New service launches with honest additional low-cardinality labels can cross eighty percent utilization without malice; MEDIUM severity should open a ticket, not a wake-up call, unless paired with growth_rate_pct_h spikes.
hardware: Linux pools running OpenTelemetry Collector and Prometheus, Kubernetes clusters emitting pod and node labels, Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud search tier indexing _metrics and prometheus-style sources, optional Observability Cloud realm for mirrored audits.
exclusions: Does not replace UC-13.5.16 SLO burn rate alerting, UC-13.5.12 detector health monitoring, UC-13.5.11 synthetic checks, UC-13.5.7 RED service map analytics, UC-13.5.10 RUM JavaScript error use cases, or UC-13.5.9 Core Web Vitals measurement. Does not automatically mutate live relabel or collector configs; it produces prioritized evidence and routing for human-approved changes.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab tenant, add a request_id label to a test metric, lower policy_max_mts for that metric in metrics_inventory.csv, ingest an hour of data, and confirm obs_uc_13_5_19_cardinality_explosion_sentinel returns HIGH or CRITICAL with non-null owner and recommended_action.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After removing the label via collector filter and restoring policy_max_mts to normal, confirm severities return to LOW or INFO and the alert wrapper emits zero rows when filtering to CRITICAL and HIGH only.

evidence: Saved search obs_uc_13_5_19_cardinality_explosion_sentinel with hourly schedule, metrics_inventory.csv governance baseline, and weekly exports archived to observability_evidence index.
